1: τυφλὸν ἐκ γενετῆς — "blind from birth." Genetē (G1079) is the noun for birth itself; ek genetēs ("out of birth") marks the condition as coextensive with the man's entire existence, not an accident that came later. The Evangelist's terse opening — "as he went along, he saw a man blind from birth" — drops the reader into the scene without prologue, the blindness stated as a fixed, absolute fact.
2: τίς ἥμαρτεν — "who sinned?" Hamartanō (G264, aorist). The disciples' question assumes a theology of strict retributive correspondence: disability is the visible result of specific sin. This view circulated widely in Second Temple Judaism (cf. the rabbinic discussion in b. Shabbat 55a: "there is no death without sin, no suffering without transgression"). The question acknowledges two logical candidates — the man himself or his parents — but cannot conceive of a third option. Jesus will supply it.
3: ἵνα φανερωθῇ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ — purpose clause: "so that the works of God might be manifested in him." Phaneroō (G5319, aorist passive subjunctive) is one of the Evangelist's signature verbs for revelation made visible (cf. John 1:31; John 2:11; John 3:21; John 17:6). The clause does not explain the cause of the man's blindness from birth but the purpose of this encounter: the condition is now the site of divine display. Ἐν αὐτῷ — "in him" — the man himself is the locus of the manifestation, not merely its occasion.
4: ἡμᾶς δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι — "we must work." The manuscripts divide between hēmas ("we/us") and eme ("me/I"), with the plural having strong support. The dei (G1163) is the impersonal verb of divine necessity used repeatedly in John (John 3:7; John 3:30; John 4:4; John 10:16; John 20:9). ἔρχεται νύξ — "night is coming" — nyx here carries both literal and theological weight: the hours of Jesus's earthly mission are limited, and the darkness that is the condition of judgment (cf. John 3:19–21) encroaches. The urgency of works follows from the finitude of the day.
5: φῶς εἰμι τοῦ κόσμου — "I am the light of the world." The ἐγώ εἰμι formula with the predicate phōs (G5457) frames the healing about to occur: Jesus is about to give literal sight to one who is literally blind, enacting in physical form what he is claiming theologically. The hotan clause ("when I am in the world") marks this as the period of his incarnate presence — the light is embodied and located. Compare John 8:12, where the same I-AM formula appears in the treasury of the temple.
6: ἔπτυσεν χαμαί, ἐποίησεν πηλόν — "he spit on the ground, made mud." Chamai (G5476) means "on the ground" or "to the earth"; it appears only here and in 18:6 in the NT. The making of mud (pēlos, G4081) from saliva and earth carries a secondary register: in Genesis 2:7, God forms (eplassen) man from the dust of the ground (choûn apo tēs gēs). The Evangelist does not invoke the parallel explicitly, but a healer who forms clay and restores sight to eyes is doing something that echoes the original act of creation. ἐπέχρισεν (G2025) — "he spread/anointed" — the verb shares its root with chriō (to anoint) and thus with Christos; the Anointed One anoints eyes that have never seen.
7: Σιλωάμ ὃ ἑρμηνεύεται Ἀπεσταλμένος — "Siloam, which is translated Sent." The pool name derives from the Hebrew/Aramaic Shiloaḥ (שִׁילֹחַ), related to the verb šālaḥ (to send). The Evangelist's gloss (hermēneuetai) activates the central "sending" theology of the Fourth Gospel: Jesus is repeatedly identified as "the one sent" (ho aposteillas me, G649); the pool named "Sent" is where the man goes at the word of the Sent One. καὶ ἦλθεν βλέπων — "and came back seeing." The aorist ēlthen and the present participle blepōn: the coming is narrated as completed, the seeing as a present ongoing state.
8: A brief narrative transition. οἱ γείτονες (hoi geitones) — "the neighbors." The community who had previously seen him begging (theōrountes, present participle — a habitual sight to them) now face an identification problem. The imperfect ἦν + present participle prosaitōn ("was begging") frames his begging as a settled, ongoing condition — what had seemed permanent is now changed.
9: ἐγώ εἰμι — "I am he." The man's self-identification uses the same formula with which Jesus discloses his divine identity throughout the Fourth Gospel (John 4:26; John 6:20; John 8:24, 28, 58; John 18:5–6). Here the usage is entirely ordinary — the man is identifying himself, not making a theological claim. The juxtaposition is deliberate: the blind man who has just received sight from Jesus now speaks with the same words Jesus uses for himself. Both uses appear within a single chapter.
10: The neighbors' question is direct: πῶς [οὖν] ἠνεῴχθησάν σου οἱ ὀφθαλμοί; — "how then were your eyes opened?" Anoigō (G455, aorist passive) — "opened." The passive voice asks who acted on his eyes without naming the actor. The question drives the three-stage interrogation that structures the rest of the chapter: neighbors (John 9:8–12), Pharisees first round (John 9:13–17), Pharisees with parents (John 9:18–23), Pharisees second round (John 9:24–34).
11: ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ λεγόμενος Ἰησοῦς — "the man called Jesus." The healed man's first identification of Jesus is by name and title: "a man called Jesus." He does not yet know more. His account is precise and factual: mud on the eyes, the command to go to Siloam, washing, sight. ὑπῆγα (aorist, "I went") / ἐνιψάμην (aorist, "I washed") / ἀνέβλεψα (aorist, "I received sight") — three completed actions in sequence, the narration matching the simplicity of obedience.
12: ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος; — "Where is that man?" Ekeinos (G1565) in the Evangelist's usage often marks a figure whose significance is greater than the speaker realizes ("that one" — with Johannine depth). Here it is simply a demonstrative pointing to an absent party. The healed man's answer — "I don't know" (ouk oida) — marks the limit of his present knowledge and sets the stage for Jesus to seek him out at the chapter's end (v35).
13: Narrative transition: the healed man is brought (agousin, G71, present — they lead/bring him) to the Pharisees. The same word is used of leading a defendant to authorities; the framing subtly positions the healed man as someone summoned for examination rather than celebrated. The Pharisees are the ones who will determine whether the healing is legally permissible.
14: ἦν δὲ σάββατον — "now it was a Sabbath." The Evangelist holds the Sabbath detail until after the healing is narrated, creating the same collision-effect as in John 5:9: the miracle is complete, the controversy begins. The making of clay (pēlon poiein) was one of the thirty-nine categories of labor prohibited on the Sabbath (Mishnah Shabbat 7:2 lists kneading); the Pharisees will focus on this infraction rather than the restoration of sight.
15: The Pharisees' direct question to the man — πῶς ἀνέβλεψας; — receives the same account he gave the neighbors, shortened: mud on eyes, washing, sight. πηλόν ἐπέθηκέν μου ἐπὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς — "he put mud upon my eyes." The verb epitithēmi (G2007, to place upon) is clinical and specific — the man does not embellish. His testimony is consistent. The short retelling through the chapter (John 9:7, 11, 15, 25) is a Johannine technique: each repetition before a new audience drives home both the simplicity and the finality of what happened.
16: σχίσμα ἐν αὐτοῖς — "a division among them." Schisma (G4978) — "split/schism." This is the same word used in 7:43 and 10:19 for the divisions Jesus produces among listeners. The logic of the split is explicit: one party argues no one from God could break the Sabbath; the other asks how a sinner could perform such signs. ἁμαρτωλὸς (G268, "sinner") vs. ποιεῖν σημεῖα τοιαῦτα ("perform such signs") — the works testify against the sinner-verdict. The works-as-witness motif from chapter 5 is alive here.
17: τί σὺ λέγεις περὶ αὐτοῦ — "what do you say about him?" The Pharisees turn the question to the man himself — a notable move. The healed man's answer is a step forward in his christological confession: προφήτης ἐστίν — "he is a prophet." From "a man called Jesus" (v11) to "a prophet" (v17). The progress will continue: at v25 he testifies to the undeniable; at v38 he worships. The chapter structures the blind man's confession as a journey from ignorance to full recognition.
18: οὐκ ἐπίστευσαν — "did not believe." The aorist marks a decisive refusal, not ongoing uncertainty. ἕως ὅτου ἐφώνησαν τοὺς γονεῖς αὐτοῦ — "until they summoned his parents." The leaders cannot deny the healing; they shift to attacking the account by challenging the identity of the healed man. Phōneō (G5455, to call, summon) is used for authoritative summons — the parents are called as witnesses in a quasi-judicial process.
19: Three questions in sequence: Is this your son? Was he born blind? How does he now see? The tripartite structure is the formal structure of witness examination. ὃν ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη — "whom you say was born blind." The leaders' framing is skeptical: they attribute the claim of congenital blindness to the parents' assertion, not yet accepting it as fact. The investigation is designed to find grounds for denial.
20: οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς ἡμῶν — "we know that this is our son." The first affirmation: identity confirmed. καὶ ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη — the second: congenital blindness confirmed. Both uses of oida (G1492, "we know") are emphatic; the parents affirm exactly what the authorities had cast in doubt. Their knowledge runs out at the third question.
21: πῶς δὲ νῦν βλέπει οὐκ οἴδαμεν — "but how he now sees we do not know." The grammar is precise: they know the "what" (blindness, sight) but not the "how" (mechanism). αὐτὸς ἡλικίαν ἔχει — "he is of age" — hēlikia (G2244) means maturity, legal adulthood. The parents deflect to their son's legal competence to testify for himself — a calculated retreat that the Evangelist immediately explains in v22.
22: ἀποσυνάγωγος (G656) — "put out of the synagogue." This compound (apo-synagōgos) appears only in the Fourth Gospel (John 9:22; John 12:42; John 16:2). Expulsion from the synagogue was a formal act of excommunication that cut the person from community worship, social belonging, and in some cases commercial life. ἤδη γὰρ συνετέθειντο οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι — "for the Jewish leaders had already agreed" — the perfect syntetheninto marks a settled, prior decision, not improvised reaction. The threat preceded this incident; the healed man's case now triggers the machinery.
23: A brief explanatory note: the parents' evasion in v21 ("he is of age, ask him") is attributed entirely to this fear. Dia touto — "for this reason" — the evasion was not ignorance but calculated self-protection under threat of expulsion. The Evangelist does not condemn them; he explains their behavior as the understandable product of institutional pressure.
24: δὸς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ — "Give glory to God." This is a formulaic oath-preamble in Jewish judicial and social settings, equivalent to "swear before God to tell the truth" (cf. Joshua 7:19, where Joshua uses it to require Achan's confession; 1 Samuel 6:5). It is not a call to praise God for the healing but a demand for truthful testimony under divine obligation. What follows — ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν — is the Pharisees' predetermined conclusion, not an open question.
25: εἰ ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν οὐκ οἶδα· ἓν οἶδα — "whether he is a sinner I do not know; one thing I know." The structure is chiastic: acknowledged uncertainty followed by declared certainty. τυφλὸς ὢν ἄρτι βλέπω — "though I was blind, now I see." The participle typhlos ōn concedes the prior state; the present blepō asserts the present reality. This is testimony reduced to its irreducible minimum: what I was, what I am now. The Pharisees cannot dislodge this because they cannot argue against the man's own experience of himself.
26: εἶπόν σοι ἤδη — "I told you already." The Evangelist records the escalating frustration on both sides: the Pharisees keep demanding the account; the healed man notes he has already given it. μήτι καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε αὐτοῦ μαθηταὶ γενέσθαι; (v27) — the counter-question, biting and ironic: "you don't also want to become his disciples, do you?" The mēti expects a negative answer but the framing exposes what is actually at stake in the investigation.
27: εἶπόν ὑμῖν ἤδη καὶ οὐκ ἠκούσατε — "I told you already and you did not listen." Ēkousate (aorist, G191) — "you listened/heard." The verb implies not just acoustic reception but receptive attention. That they did not "hear" despite being told is the same diagnosis Jesus made of the religious leaders in chapters 5–8: they have ears but do not hear. μήτι καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε αὐτοῦ μαθηταὶ γενέσθαι; — the man's rhetoric has sharpened from factual testimony to pointed challenge, marking his growing confidence.
28: σὺ μαθητὴς εἶ ἐκείνου, ἡμεῖς δὲ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐσμεν μαθηταί — "you are that man's disciple; we are disciples of Moses." The contrast is drawn in discipleship terms. Ekeinou ("of that one") is dismissive — they refuse to name Jesus. The self-identification with Moses is ironic in light of John 5:45–47, where Jesus told these same leaders that Moses would be their accuser and that Moses wrote about him. The Mosaic credential they claim is the very thing that testifies against their rejection of Jesus.
29: ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι Μωϋσεῖ λελάληκεν ὁ θεός — "we know that God has spoken to Moses." The perfect lelālēken marks an abiding fact: God's speech to Moses is completed and stands. τοῦτον δὲ οὐκ οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν — "but this man — we do not know where he is from." The Johannine irony reaches its peak: the reader knows exactly where Jesus is from (from above, from the Father, from God — cf. John 7:27–29; John 8:14). The leaders' professed ignorance about his origin is the precise point at which they fail.
30: ἐν τούτῳ γὰρ τὸ θαυμαστόν ἐστιν — "in this is the remarkable thing." Thaumaston (G2298) — "remarkable, marvelous, astonishing." The healed man's irony is sharp: they claim not to know where this man is from, yet he opened the eyes of one born blind — which, as v32 will note, has no precedent in recorded history. The man's argument is empirical: results this unprecedented require a source this extraordinary.
31: οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἁμαρτωλῶν ὁ θεὸς οὐκ ἀκούει — "we know that God does not hear sinners." The man draws on common theological reasoning (cf. Psalms 66:18; Proverbs 15:29): God's receptiveness to prayer is conditioned on the petitioner's moral standing. ἀλλ᾽ ἐάν τις θεοσεβὴς ᾖ καὶ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιῇ — "but if someone is devout toward God and does his will." The man's argument is a syllogism: God answers this man's acts; God does not hear sinners; therefore this man is not a sinner. The logic is the leaders' own theology turned against their conclusion.
32: ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος οὐκ ἠκούσθη — "from eternity it has not been heard." Ek tou aiōnos — "from the beginning of the age" — a hyperbolic claim of absolute uniqueness. That the eyes of a person born blind should be opened has no parallel in healing narratives the man can cite. The unprecedented nature of the sign is itself the argument for the unprecedented character of the one who performed it.
33: εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος παρὰ θεοῦ, οὐκ ἠδύνατο ποιεῖν οὐδέν — "if this man were not from God, he could do nothing." The conditional (ei mē ēn...ouk ēdynato) is second-class (contrary to fact in form): the healed man is not raising a hypothesis but asserting a logical necessity. Para theou ("from God") — the same phrase the leaders denied (v16, "not from God"); the healed man asserts it as the only adequate explanation. The chapter's irony is complete: the man born blind sees clearly; those who see are blind (v39–41).
34: ἐν ἁμαρτίαις σὺ ἐγεννήθης ὅλος — "in sins you were born wholly." The leaders deploy the retributive theology of v2 — which Jesus had explicitly rejected — as a weapon against the man who challenged them. Holos (G3650, "wholly/completely") is emphatic: the charge is total condemnation. ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω — "they cast him out." Ekballō (G1544, aorist) is the same verb used for casting out demons and for the expulsion Jesus warned about in v22. The man is formally expelled from the synagogue — the institutional consequence his parents feared has fallen on him instead.
35: ἤκουσεν Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω — "Jesus heard that they had cast him out." The Evangelist uses akouō (G191, heard) — Jesus is informed. εὑρὼν αὐτόν — "having found him." The word heuriskō (G2147) — to find — carries the weight of the divine search here: the one expelled from human community is sought out by Jesus. The connection to Ezekiel 34 (the shepherd seeking the lost sheep) is active in the background. υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — the full Danielic title (G5207 + G444), unlike the anarthrous form in John 5:27.
36: τίς ἐστιν, κύριε, ἵνα πιστεύσω εἰς αὐτόν; — "Who is he, Lord, so that I may believe in him?" The man addresses Jesus with kyrie (G2962) — "Sir" or "Lord." The construction hina pisteusō (purpose clause, G4100, aorist subjunctive) indicates his readiness: he does not resist belief but lacks the object. The question mirrors the Samaritan woman's posture before Jesus's self-disclosure in John 4:25–26.
37: καὶ ἑώρακας αὐτόν, καὶ ὁ λαλῶν μετὰ σοῦ ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν — "you have both seen him, and the one speaking with you is he." The perfect heōrakas ("you have seen and the seeing stands") followed by the present ho lalōn ("the one speaking") — sight and speech converge in the same moment. Jesus identifies himself not with the divine egō eimi of 4:26 but with a gentle, direct pointing: "that one — he is the one speaking with you."
38: κύριε, πιστεύω — "Lord, I believe." The transition from kyrie as polite address (v36) to kyrie as confession is complete in the act of προσεκύνησεν αὐτῷ — "he worshiped him." Proskyneō (G4352) is the verb used in John for worship of God (John 4:20–24; John 12:20). The man born blind ends his day cast out of the synagogue and worshiping Jesus. The Evangelist does not need to comment: the progression from "a man called Jesus" (v11) → "a prophet" (v17) → "Lord" (v36) → worship (v38) speaks for itself.
39: εἰς κρίμα ἐγὼ εἰς τὸν κόσμον τοῦτον ἦλθον — "for judgment I have come into this world." Krima (G2917) here is the realized verdict — the actual outcome of the process — rather than the juridical proceeding (krisis). The purpose clause that follows — "that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind" — expresses the inversion John 9 has dramatized: the blind man sees; the Pharisees, who claim to see, are exposed as blind. The judgment is not imposed externally; it is the sorting that occurs in the encounter with the light (cf. John 3:19–21).
40: Some Pharisees who were with Jesus hear his pronouncement on blindness and take his statement as applying to themselves: μὴ καὶ ἡμεῖς τυφλοί ἐσμεν; — "Surely we are not blind too, are we?" The particle mē expects a negative answer — they are confident they are not blind. Their question reveals they heard Jesus's words but did not grasp that "those who see" and "those who are blind" are categories determined by response to the light, not by physical sight.
41: εἰ τυφλοὶ ἦτε, οὐκ ἂν εἴχετε ἁμαρτίαν — "if you were blind, you would not have sin." The second-class condition (contrary-to-fact): their actual seeing is the problem, not blindness. Ignorance without opportunity for knowledge carries a different moral weight. νῦν δὲ λέγετε ὅτι βλέπομεν, ἡ ἁμαρτία ὑμῶν μένει — "but now you say 'we see,' your sin remains." Menei (G3306, present, "abides/remains") — the Johannine verb of permanent dwelling applied to sin. Their claim to sight, combined with their refusal to see who Jesus is, constitutes their guilt and makes it permanent.
